does: This paper identifies and addresses the problem that standard LLM alignment procedures—supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL)—actually increase hallucination rather than improve factual accuracy. The authors conduct empirical analysis to pinpoint specific causes: SFT on novel training data encourages the model to generate false facts when encountering unfamiliar content, while RL reward functions prioritize helpfulness and verbosity over accuracy, inadvertently rewarding plausible-sounding but incorrect responses. Based on these findings, the paper proposes factuality-aware alignment methods that modify both the SFT and RL stages to explicitly account for and penalize hallucination, thereby improving factual correctness while maintaining instruction-following capabilities.
connects: This work extends recent research on LLM hallucination and the limitations of standard alignment techniques like RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback). It builds on prior observations that scaling and instruction-tuning can paradoxically increase false outputs, and connects to broader discussions about trade-offs between helpfulness and truthfulness in aligned models. The paper responds to the RLHF paradigm established by InstructGPT and subsequent work showing that reward models optimized for human preferences don't necessarily optimize for factuality. It also relates to concurrent research examining how fine-tuning on diverse data affects model behavior and the role of knowledge boundaries in preventing hallucination.
enables: This work opens directions for developing alignment procedures that jointly optimize multiple objectives—helpfulness, harmlessness, and factuality—rather than treating them as orthogonal concerns. It enables future research into factuality-aware reward modeling, training data curation strategies that respect model knowledge boundaries, and evaluation frameworks that measure hallucination alongside standard alignment metrics. The paper suggests pathways toward more reliable AI assistants that maintain instruction-following without sacrificing accuracy, and could inform the design of alignment techniques that are aware of what a model does and does not know. This foundation supports development of safer, more trustworthy LLMs for high-stakes applications.
